title: Probing Spacetime Topology and Superposition with Accelerated Detectors
authors: P. Poopathysankar, Lucas Hackl, Anwesha Chakraborty
abstract: We study entanglement harvested by Unruh DeWitt detectors following Rindler trajectories in compactified and superposed Minkowski spacetime. We consider different directions of acceleration (both parallel and antiparallel), separation between detectors and direction of spatial compactification mutually perpendicular to each other. Using the standard entanglement harvesting protocol, we analyze how these features influence the extracted correlations. When detector separation is perpendicular to the direction of acceleration, the harvested entanglement is uniformly suppressed due to increased spacelike separation. Compactification enhances field correlations leading to an increased concurrence and an extended harvesting range at higher accelerations. Additionally, we show that spacetime superposition introduces interference effects that further enlarge the entanglement harvesting region in parameter space, particularly in the high acceleration regime. We also find that the effect of antiparallel acceleration yielding significantly higher entanglement than parallel acceleration prevails in compactified and superposed spacetime.
summary: This paper explores how the geometric properties of spacetime, such as periodic dimensions and quantum superpositions of geometries, influence the ability of accelerated probes to harvest entanglement from a vacuum field. It demonstrates that topological features can protect entanglement against the noise induced by the Unruh effect. The findings suggest that entanglement harvesting could serve as an operational tool to detect quantum features of spacetime.
main_problem: The study investigates how the interplay of relativistic acceleration, spacetime topology (compactification), and quantum superposition of geometries affects the extraction of entanglement from a quantum field.
main_result: Compactification and spacetime superposition both expand the parameter space for entanglement harvesting, particularly in high-acceleration regimes, while anti-parallel acceleration yields higher entanglement than parallel acceleration.
method: The authors use the Unruh-DeWitt entanglement harvesting protocol within a perturbative framework, calculating Wightman functions in quotient Rindler spacetimes and their superpositions.
model_or_system: Two-level Unruh-DeWitt detectors following Rindler trajectories (parallel and anti-parallel) interacting with a massless scalar field in both compactified and superposed Minkowski/Rindler geometries.
key_observables: Concurrence, Negativity, local excitation probability (noise), and cross-correlation terms.
important_parameters: Acceleration (a), detector separation (R), compactification length (L), detector energy gap (Omega), and switching function width (sigma).
main_assumptions: Weak coupling limit, Gaussian switching function, massless scalar field, and untwisted field boundary conditions.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows detector trajectories; Figure 2 plots concurrence across different spacetime configurations; Figure 3 presents a phase diagram of the entanglement harvesting region; Figure 4 shows concurrence as a function of compactification length and acceleration.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of entanglement harvesting in complex geometries, defines the mathematical model of detectors and spacetime, presents numerical results for various acceleration and topology configurations, and concludes with the physical implications for probing spacetime features.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Accelerated detector trajectories. Here we represent three out of the four dimensions of Minkowski space (x, y, t) and the detector separation in the y-direction. The blue curves represent the trajectories of detector A while the red curve represents the trajectory of detector B. The left panel represents the parallel acceleration case while the right panel represents the anti-parallel acceleration case.

FIG. 2. Concurrence C for accelerated detectors in various spacetime geometry. Panels (a) and (b) show the parallel acceleration configuration, while panels (c) and (d) show the anti-parallel configuration. The left column presents C as a function of the energy gap Ωand detector separation R for constant acceleration a = 0.5, whereas the right column presents C as a function of Ωand acceleration a for constant detector separation R = 0.2. All figures are shown for four spacetime configurations i.e., Rindler spacetime, compactified Rindler spacetime with L = 0.25 and L = 0.75, and the symmetric superposition of the two compactified branches. The plots show that both range and region of...

FIG. 3. Region of Concurrence in the (Ω, a) parameter space. The shaded regions indicate the parameter domains in which the concurrence C is nonzero for different spacetime configu- rations for the case of parallel acceleration corresponding to figure2b. The entanglement region is larger in compactified Rindler spacetime (blue and green) than in ordinary Rindler spacetime (red), and is further enlarged in the symmetric su- perposition of the two compactified Rindler branches (yellow).

FIG. 4. Concurrence C as a function of compactification length L and acceleration a. The plots illustrate the combined effect of acceleration and compactification. (a) corresponds to parallel acceleration showing the higher concurrence value for small compactification length and low acceleration which becomes progressively weaker at higher compactification length and acceleration. (b) corresponds to anti-parallel acceleration where concurrence is larger for small L but high value of acceleration in comparison to parallel acceleration scenario.
